title: What is a Database?
intro: You've been hired to build a simple to-do list app. The founder asks 'Where should we store the tasks?' Think of it like choosing between a shoebox, a filing cabinet, or a professional warehouse for your data -- each has very different capabilities. Let's explore the options and understand what a database actually is.

teach.title: Different Storage Options
teach.body: Storing data is like storing physical items. You could use a **shoebox** (a text file -- simple but messy), a **spreadsheet binder** (a structured file like CSV -- organized but limited), or a **professional warehouse** (a database -- organized, searchable, secure, and built to scale).

teach.title: The Restaurant Kitchen
teach.body: Think of a database like the kitchen in a busy restaurant. You (the app) are the waiter taking orders. You don't cook the food yourself -- you hand the order slip to the **kitchen** (the database), which has all the equipment, ingredients, and trained staff to handle it. The kitchen can process dozens of orders at once without mixing them up, and if the power flickers, it doesn't lose track of what was ordered. That's what a database does for your data.

teach.title: What IS a Database, Exactly?
teach.body:
A database is a **software program** that runs on a server and manages data for you. Popular ones include:

1. **PostgreSQL** -- powerful, open-source, great for most apps
2. **MySQL** -- very popular, used by WordPress and many web apps
3. **SQLite** -- lightweight, stores data in a single file (great for small apps)
4. **MongoDB** -- stores data as flexible documents instead of tables

Your app code sends commands to the database, and it handles the storage, retrieval, and safety of the data.

teach.title: How Your App Uses a Database
teach.body:
Your app doesn't open files directly. Instead:

1. Your app **connects** to the database (like dialing a phone number)
2. Your app **sends commands** in SQL (a special language for databases)
3. The database **executes** the command and sends results back
4. Your app **uses the results** (displays them, processes them, etc.)

The database is a separate process from your app -- they communicate over a connection.

Understanding what a database is puts you ahead of most vibecoding beginners. Every app that makes money -- from SaaS tools to marketplaces -- stores user data, payments, and subscriptions in a database. This is the foundation of building apps that handle real users and real revenue.

A database is a specialized program that stores and manages data. Your app connects to it and sends commands in SQL. The database handles all the complex stuff: fast lookups, safe writes, and multiple users.

start.narrative: You're building a to-do app. The founder says: 'Let's keep it simple -- just save the tasks in a JSON file on the server.' This works at first with 5 test users. But then you launch and get 500 users. Problems start appearing: some users' tasks disappear randomly. What's most likely happening?

choices.text: Two users are writing to the file at the same time, overwriting each other's data
The JSON file is getting too big and slowing down
The server is running out of memory

partial-size.narrative: File size could matter eventually, but 500 users' worth of to-dos is only a few megabytes -- not big enough to cause data loss. The real problem is concurrency: multiple users writing to the same file at the same time, overwriting each other's changes.

choices.text: So it's a race condition -- two writes happening at once

wrong-memory.narrative: Memory usually isn't the issue for 500 users. A JSON file with task data would be tiny in memory. The problem is more fundamental: the file can't handle multiple users writing to it at the same time. When two writes overlap, data gets lost.

choices.text: Got it -- concurrent writes are causing data loss

diagnose-fix.narrative: Confirmed: it's a race condition. The founder asks 'OK, what should we use instead?' Pick the right replacement.

choices.text: A real database like PostgreSQL or SQLite -- they handle concurrent writes safely with transactions
Just add file locking so only one user writes at a time -- everyone else queues
Save each user's tasks in a separate file so writes never overlap

pick-engine.narrative: You agree on a database. The team is small, the app is single-server, and you want to ship today. Which engine do you start with?

choices.text: SQLite -- single file, zero setup, transactions out of the box, easy to migrate to Postgres later
PostgreSQL -- start with the production-grade option even if it means setting up a separate DB server
MongoDB -- to-do items are document-shaped, schemaless will be faster

design-schema.narrative: Time to design the data. You need to store tasks belonging to users. What's your minimum viable schema?

choices.text: Two tables: users (id, email) and tasks (id, user_id, title, done, created_at) with user_id as a foreign key
One tasks table with a username TEXT column instead of a user_id foreign key
Skip the schema -- store everything as a JSON blob in a single 'data' column

scale-up.narrative: You ship and the disappearing-task bug vanishes. Three months later you have 50,000 users and the page that loads a user's tasks has slowed from 50ms to 800ms. What's your next move?

choices.text: EXPLAIN the slow query, add an index on tasks(user_id), and re-measure -- if SQLite then becomes the bottleneck, migrate to PostgreSQL
Migrate straight to PostgreSQL -- bigger DB will be faster
Buy a faster server -- the database is fine, the hardware is the problem

end-good.narrative: EXPLAIN shows a full table scan on tasks. You add CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_user_id ON tasks(user_id) and the query drops to 4ms. SQLite handles 50,000 users comfortably. Six months and one Postgres migration later, you're at 500,000 users with the same data model and the same indexes. You measured before you migrated, so every step was justified.
end-good.feedback: You walked the natural progression: flat file -> SQLite -> indexed SQLite -> PostgreSQL, with each step driven by a measured problem instead of a guess. That's how real apps evolve their storage. Premature migration to a 'bigger' database without an index would have hidden the actual bottleneck behind a much more expensive change.

end-mediocre.narrative: You spend a weekend migrating to PostgreSQL. The query goes from 800ms to 600ms -- faster, but not by much, because the underlying problem is still a full scan on a 5-million-row table. You eventually add the index anyway. The migration was real work, just not the work that actually mattered.
end-mediocre.feedback: Migrating engines without first understanding the bottleneck is a common, expensive mistake. EXPLAIN is free, indexes are cheap, and either change is reversible -- always exhaust them before committing to a migration that touches every connection in your app.

end-weak.narrative: The faster server costs 4x as much per month and the slow page now takes 700ms instead of 800ms. The CPU graph is flat -- the bottleneck was never CPU, it was a missing index doing a full table scan. You're now paying more every month for a problem that an index would have fixed for free.
end-weak.feedback: Throwing hardware at a database problem is the most expensive way to get the smallest improvement. Slowness in a database is almost always about missing indexes, bad query plans, or N+1 queries -- not raw CPU. Always profile before you provision.
